title: Spinning Across Scales
text:
The question was never about cup size. It was about what her body was for.

I often say that without tennis, I might never have finished my Ph.D.
During my years at the University of Florida, tennis gave me much more than exercise. It gave me a community when I had none. It became my way of surviving the loneliness and pressure of graduate school. Many of those friends remain among my closest friends today.
Perhaps that is why I have always paid attention to stories from the tennis world.
Not only to championships.
But to the lives behind them.
Among all the players I have admired, one story has stayed with me for years.
It was not about winning a Grand Slam.
It was not about extraordinary talent.
It was about a Romanian player who made an unusual decision.
She underwent breast reduction surgery.
Not because of illness.
Not because of appearance.
But because she believed her body was preventing her from becoming the player she wanted to be.
After the first surgery, her ranking climbed from outside the Top 100 into the Top 100.
A year later, she chose a second reduction.
Soon she entered the Top 20.
Eventually, she became World No. 1.
I have no idea whether surgery alone changed her career.
That isn’t the point.
The story stayed with me because it quietly asked a question that reaches far beyond tennis.
What is your body for?
Growing up, I rarely heard that question.
Instead, I heard another one.
How should a woman look?
For a long time, I thought the answer was obvious.
Slimmer.
Fairer.
More feminine.
More attractive.
The body existed, consciously or unconsciously, to satisfy someone else’s expectations.
Years later, I realized that elite sport asks an entirely different question.
Not:

How attractive can my body become?

But:

What kind of body allows me to become the person I want to be?

Those are two completely different optimization problems.
One optimizes appearance.
The other optimizes purpose.
For a brief moment, I thought the conversation around women’s bodies had begun to change.
Strength became fashionable.
Women were encouraged to lift weights instead of simply losing weight.
I welcomed it.
Then, almost without anyone noticing, “strength” quietly became another aesthetic standard.
Now the ideal woman was expected to have visible abs.
The body had changed.
The gaze had not.
People were still looking at women.
They were simply looking for different things.
Whenever I hear people celebrating visible strength, I cannot help smiling.
I often joke with my friends:

A scientist’s cerebral cortex is a scientist’s six-pack.

Not every kind of strength can be photographed.
Some strength is built by asking difficult questions for years.
Some by staying curious when certainty is easier.
Some by changing your mind when the evidence changes.
Those muscles are invisible.
Yet they shape the world far more than the visible ones.
Perhaps this is why that tennis story has never really been about tennis.
Nor has it been about surgery.
It has always been about purpose.
Every society teaches us what to optimize.
Beauty.
Youth.
Productivity.
Strength.
Achievement.
Very few ask a more fundamental question:

What kind of life are you trying to build?

Because once you know the answer,
your body,
your career,
your relationships,
and even your ambitions
begin to reorganize themselves around it.
Perhaps freedom is not the ability to become anything we want.
Perhaps freedom begins when we are finally allowed to choose what we are optimizing for.

title: Essays
text:
Thinking Across Scales




Ideas that emerged from journeys, research, and life.











The landscapes taught me to observe.
The essays taught me to connect.


What begins in a place often travels into a system. These essays trace that movement—from field observation to ecological thinking, from one scale to another, and from one question to a wider pattern.































Featured Essays














Systems



How attention shifts when a landscape is read as a living network of relations rather than a fixed backdrop.
Read Essay →











Observation



A reflection on perception, pattern, and the way landscapes reveal structure over time.
Read Essay →






Movement



An exploration of movement, recursion, and the forms of thinking that loop back on themselves.
Read Essay →




















Still growing




Each new piece extends the work of looking closely, connecting widely, and following the patterns that emerge.

title: Fall of A Tulip Trees
text:
It had survived the ice ages of the Quaternary, only to fall beneath the steel teeth of an excavator.
A stand of wild Chinese tulip trees (Liriodendron chinense), an ancient magnolia lineage and a nationally protected species in China, had been cleared to make way for a road leading to a wind farm. Nearby, another protected species, Magnolia officinalis var. biloba, faced an uncertain future.  At first glance, the question seemed obvious: should we save the road or save the trees?
But standing there, I realized that was the wrong question.
Was renewable energy always sustainable? Not necessarily.
Was this simply a conflict between economic development and environmental protection? I no longer believed that either.
Something deeper was unfolding beneath the surface.
I arrived at the site in a vehicle provided by the state-owned wind power company itself.
They were proud of the road we were driving on—it had been built by their company.
As an investigator, however, I could not ignore the irony.
The people leading the investigation were also the people being investigated.
Every destination depended on their guidance. Every conversation happened on their terms.
Long before I reached the forest, I had already begun wondering whether independent observation was even possible.
During every break, I wandered over to talk with the construction workers.
I never saw them as “ordinary laborers.”
If anything, they were the anonymous builders carrying the weight of the country’s ambitions on their shoulders.
I photographed them as they assembled turbines against the mountains.
One worker smiled when I promised to send him the photos so he could show his family.
For a moment, I wondered why he had never taken these pictures himself.
Then the answer came quietly.
By the end of the day, work had already consumed everything.
Survival leaves little room for poetry.

title: Becoming an Ecologist
text:
This is not simply a record of positions held or papers published.
It is the story of how one landscape led to another, how one question became many, and how a career gradually evolved from studying ecosystems to exploring the ecology of the mind.

title: Thinking Across Scales
text:
In the summer of 2026, I was preparing an application for an interdisciplinary art and science residency. The application required something I had never assembled before: a portfolio—not of publications, but of ideas.
For years, my work had been scattered across scientific papers, conference presentations, field notebooks, software prototypes, photographs, and unfinished essays. They all described different parts of the same journey, yet none of them revealed the whole.
The residency application became a catalyst.
Not because I wanted another website, but because I realized I needed a place where landscapes, science, computation, writing, and personal reflection could finally exist in conversation with one another.
What began as a portfolio gradually evolved into something much larger.
Thinking Across Scales.
This website was not planned over years.
It was built over two weeks.
What once required months of web development can now be built through modern open-source tools, AI-assisted development, and reproducible publishing workflows.
The technology accelerated the construction.
The ideas had been developing for decades.
Built with:

Quarto
GitHub
Mapbox
Markdown
Git
AI-assisted development
Cloud-based workflow

The website itself became another field experiment—an exploration of how modern computational tools can expand the practice of scientific communication.

title: About
text:
Preface


About

The story behind Thinking Across Scales.





This website did not begin as a website. It began as a need to gather ideas that had long existed across landscapes, notebooks, code, and unfinished drafts.










The Origin Story


A residency application became the catalyst.
In the summer of 2026, while preparing an application for an interdisciplinary art and science residency, I encountered an unexpected requirement: a portfolio—not of publications, but of ideas.
For years, the work had been dispersed across scientific papers, field notes, conference talks, code experiments, photographs, and fragments of essays. Each piece was real, but none of them could hold the whole.
That application became a turning point. What started as a practical response to one form quickly became a deeper editorial question: how can landscapes, science, computation, and reflection live in one continuous conversation?
What began as a portfolio gradually evolved into something much larger.





“Not because I wanted another website, but because I needed a place where ideas from different scales could finally meet.”




“The residency deadline was immediate. The underlying inquiry had already been alive for years.”








“The implementation took only two weeks. ”The ideas had been developing for two decades.”







Building the Website


Not a website project—an experiment in modern scientific publishing.
The technical build moved quickly because the ecosystem has changed. Instead of a traditional, months-long web build, this project grew through an open, composable workflow:

Quarto for reproducible, publication-ready writing
Markdown as the primary authoring layer
Git and GitHub for transparent version control
Mapbox for place-based, spatial storytelling
AI-assisted development for rapid prototyping and iteration
Cloud-based publishing for continuous updates

Technology accelerated implementation, but the website’s structure emerged from a much longer arc of fieldwork, research, and writing practice. In that sense, the build process itself became part of the research: a live test of how open-source tools can expand scientific communication.




Workflow Notes


Open-source workflow Editorial publishing Reproducible writing Computational storytelling










About Me


Scientist, writer, and systems thinker.
Jing Yuan is an environmental scientist and writer working at the intersection of ecological research, computational methods, and public communication.
Current position: Environmental scientist and interdisciplinary researcher focused on connecting landscape inquiry with data-driven storytelling.
Research interests include Landscape Ecology, Remote Sensing, Environmental Data Science, and Science Communication.




The work moves between field observation, quantitative analysis, design, and narrative—always with attention to scale.










An Intellectual Ecosystem


No idea grows in isolation.
Thinking Across Scales is an original synthesis, but it did not emerge in isolation. Like every ecosystem, ideas evolve through relationships—with mentors, books, places, and conversations. This section acknowledges a few of the people and works that continue to shape the way I think.




An editorial acknowledgement of relationships that continue to shape the work, rather than a formal bibliography.






People


Matt Cohen
My advisor in research methodology and scientific thinking. He fundamentally shaped how I formulate questions, approach research, and pursue scholarship.
Learn More →
Qinqin Liu
Interdisciplinary artist. Introduced me to interdisciplinary art and expanded my understanding of how science and art can exist in dialogue.
Learn More →
Daoist Philosophy
A lifelong influence rather than a single person. Reminds me that systems are dynamic, relational, and always becoming rather than fixed.




Books


Philip Ball
Placeholder for future description.
C. S. “Buzz” Holling
Placeholder for future description.










Academic Profile →


This section supports credentials; it does not lead the story.



→ Curriculum Vitae
→ Google Scholar
→ ORCID
→ LinkedIn









The Journey Continues


An evolving collection, not a finished archive.
Thinking Across Scales continues to grow as a living publication: landscapes, ideas, essays, field notes, maps, and computational tools assembled over time. Each addition is less a “new page” than another movement in an ongoing inquiry—how we observe, interpret, and communicate across scales.

title: The Cost After the Numbers
text:
Walking alone through a mining landscape in Inner Mongolia. A narrow trail separates native vegetation from towering piles of mining waste. Against the immense scale of the altered landscape, I appear almost insignificant, standing at the intersection of individual witness, organized extraction, and society’s responsibility to restore what has been lost.




I thought I was calculating ecological damage. I didn’t realize I was also calculating the weight of scientific responsibility.

Seven and a half hours.
Twenty-six kilometers.
Across mountains scarred by illegal mining.
When I returned from the field, I thought the most difficult part of the investigation was behind me.
I was wrong.
The hardest part began after I submitted the report.

The landscape itself carried the memory of a development philosophy once proudly summarized by a local saying:

“Better to choke than to starve.”

Economic growth at any cost.
Factories before forests.
Production before consequences.
Walking those mountains, I wasn’t simply measuring ecological damage.
I was walking through the physical remains of an idea.
Every abandoned road.
Every excavated hillside.
Every dry stream.
Asked the same question.



At first glance, these barren hills appear to be nothing more than yellow earth. But just beneath the surface lies a shallow coal seam. Because the coal is so close to the ground, extraction requires relatively little equipment, making unauthorized mining possible even in remote mountains.


What happens when today’s survival becomes tomorrow’s debt?

Weeks later, after the field survey, calculations, and endless revisions, I finally submitted the ecological damage assessment report.
Only then did I fully understand what I had actually written.
It was no longer just a scientific report.
It was evidence.
The number I calculated—the monetary value of ecological damage—would become part of a courtroom decision.
It might determine another person’s sentence.
At that moment, I felt a responsibility I had never encountered during six years of scientific training.
I had learned how to design research.
How to collect data.
How to quantify uncertainty.
No one had taught me what it meant when science entered the courtroom.

The burden was not only legal.
It was scientific.
Ecological damage assessment was still a young discipline.
The methods were evolving.
Many assumptions remained uncertain.
Yet the legal system required something science rarely promises:
A precise number.
A final answer.
As if uncertainty itself could be reduced to a single figure.
For the first time in my career, I wondered whether my report deserved the authority it had been given.



What appears to be an empty yellow hillside is, in fact, a shallow coal deposit. Here, the boundary between geology and economics is only a few meters thick. When the resource lies so close to the surface, even remote mountains become vulnerable to unauthorized extraction.



Not long afterward, I received notice that I might be asked to testify in court.
I refused.
Not because I doubted my work.
But because I realized the report no longer belonged entirely to science.
It had entered another world.
A world where evidence, law, politics, and power intersected in ways I could neither control nor fully understand.
I also began to worry that the report could become something I had never intended it to be.
A weapon.
Not only against environmental destruction.
But perhaps within political struggles hidden far beyond my field investigation.
Whether that fear was justified almost became secondary.
The realization itself changed me.

That investigation marked the first time I understood that scientific neutrality is not something a scientist simply possesses.
It is something we struggle to protect.
Sometimes successfully.
Sometimes not.
Science may begin with curiosity.
But once it leaves our hands, it acquires consequences that no statistical model can predict.

I still believe natural resources damage should be investigated.
I still believe science belongs in public decision-making.
But since that investigation, I have carried a different question into every project.
It is no longer simply:

“Is my analysis scientifically rigorous?”

It is also:

“Am I prepared for what happens after the science leaves my hands?”

Perhaps the hardest part of becoming a scientist is not learning how to calculate uncertainty.
It is learning how to live with responsibility.



A blast hole drilled for illegal mining, shown here with my hand for scale. Some explosions begin long before the detonation.

title: White Terror of the Qinghai Rhododendrons
text:
Had it not been for the white rhododendrons blanketing the mountainside, the snow lingering on the peaks of the Qilian Range, and the crystalline air of four thousand meters, I might have left with the same emptiness carved into the riverbanks and hollowed out of the mountains themselves.
Policies descended from above.
Cigarette butts accumulated below. 
I walked across a mound of illegally excavated gravel, measuring distances that would later become estimates in a damage assessment report.
A local official waited nearby.
Small in stature, weathered by the plateau, and speaking with a thick local accent, he never stopped smoking.
One cigarette after another.
We were standing more than four thousand meters above sea level.
I could not help wondering how his lungs endured what the altitude had already made scarce.
In recent years, environmental policies had arrived one after another.
Ecological redlines.
Ecological compensation.
Green GDP.
Each new directive burned brightly for a day, much like the cigarette between his fingers.
Then the smoke drifted away.
The consequences, however, remained for a lifetime.
We climbed higher into the valley.
Snow still rested on the ridges above us.
The shaded slopes were covered by carpets of Qinghai rhododendrons in full bloom.
Their pale blossoms seemed to exist for beauty alone.
Then, around the bend, another quarry appeared.
An entire mountainside had been sliced away.
Standing there, I felt as though something had been cut from me as well.

title: Responsibility Determines Scale
text:
Responsibility Determines Scale Why We See Different Problems
An unfinished note on leadership, communication, and innovation.
On a Friday afternoon, after spending nearly a year developing a proposal for infrastructure innovation, I finally had thirty minutes to present it.
It wasn’t a large audience—just an internal meeting with colleagues and managers from different branches.
I spoke with everything I had. I explained the workflow bottlenecks, the architecture, the pilot, and the long-term vision.
When I finished, I looked around the room.
Nothing.
No excitement.
No resistance.
No curiosity.
Only the same calm expressions I had seen a year earlier when I first shared the idea.
After the meeting, I drove to the American River Parkway and walked for nearly an hour.
I wasn’t asking why the proposal had failed.
I was asking a different question.
How could we look at the same proposal, yet see entirely different things?
It would have been easy to blame bureaucracy. Bureaucracy certainly mattered. But the longer I walked, the less convinced I became that it was the deepest explanation.
Instead, I began to suspect that the real difference was scale.
My proposal was asking a root question:
Why is the system designed this way in the first place?
The people around me were asking different questions:
Will this affect our current workflow? How much will it cost? Do we have the staff? Can we deliver this on time?
None of these questions were wrong.
They simply belonged to different scales.
Over time I realized something important.
People are not necessarily looking at the same problem and reaching different conclusions.
They are often looking at different scales of the same reality.
A supervisor sees a team.
A branch manager sees a branch.
An executive sees an organization.
Responsibility expands some horizons while inevitably narrowing others.
Responsibility determines scale.
Scale determines what becomes visible.
That realization changed the way I communicate.
I used to believe that when people didn’t understand an idea, I simply needed to provide more context.
Now I think the challenge is different.
Context explains.
Scale aligns.
Before discussing solutions, I first try to align the scale from which we are observing the problem.
Innovation is rarely rejected because people lack intelligence. More often, it is because they are looking from different scales of responsibility. Before changing minds, we must first align scales.

title: Thinking Across Scales
text:
Every landscape is also a way of thinking.

We often explain problems at the scale of individuals, when their roots lie in larger systems. We debate policies at the national level, yet rarely ask how they are lived by a single person. Thinking Across Scales is the practice of moving continuously between these perspectives—between individuals and institutions, places and planets, moments and millennia. Ecological problems are fundamentally problems of scale. I have come to believe that life is, too.
I did not arrive at this idea through philosophy.
I arrived through fieldwork.
Years of ecological investigations across China’s forests, wetlands, grasslands, and mountains slowly revealed a recurring pattern.
The questions I carried into the field were rarely the questions I brought home.
A fallen tulip tree became a story about institutions. A cigarette butt became a story about policy. A wetland became a story about time.
The Ecology of the Mind is my attempt to continue these conversations.
It is not a travel blog.
It is not an academic website.
It is a living collection of journeys, essays, conversations, and ideas exploring how landscapes shape the ways we think—and how our ways of thinking, in turn, reshape the landscapes we inhabit.
This is where the journey begins.

title: The Five Elements of Leadership
text:
Leadership · Reflection · Public Speaking




A graduation address for the California Department of Water Resources Emerging Leaders Cohort 2024


Leadership is often described in the language of strategy, influence, or decision-making. But over time I have found it more useful to think of leadership as an ecology: a set of conditions that shape how people grow, how trust travels, and how courage becomes collective.

title: The Five Elements of Leadership
text:
Introduction
After reading Trust and Inspire, I found myself returning to a much older framework: the Five Elements in Chinese philosophy—Metal, Water, Fire, Earth, and Wood. These elements are not merely symbols of the natural world. They are also patterns of character, relationship, and transformation. They describe how strength is formed, how movement gathers, how warmth is shared, how stability is offered, and how growth becomes possible.
Seen through that lens, leadership is not a fixed style. It is a practice of balance. At different moments, a leader must clarify, soften, kindle, steady, and cultivate. The most trusted leaders are not defined by one trait alone; they are remembered for knowing what a moment requires, and for creating the conditions in which others can flourish.

title: The Five Elements of Leadership
text:
Metal: Clarity Under Pressure
Metal suggests refinement through pressure. It brings to mind the leader whose judgment becomes sharper not in comfort, but in difficulty. When uncertainty rises, this kind of leader does not add noise. They cut through confusion with steadiness, discernment, and a willingness to make decisions that are both clear and principled.
But Metal is not hardness for its own sake. Its deeper lesson is integrity. A leader forged by experience learns that clarity is not only about being decisive; it is about aligning action with values, especially when the path forward is not obvious.

title: The Five Elements of Leadership
text:
Water: Quiet Strength
Water teaches a different kind of power. It is patient, adaptive, and often underestimated. It does not insist on force, yet it changes landscapes over time. In leadership, Water appears as the capacity to listen, to nurture, and to move with circumstances rather than against them.
An inspiring leader knows when to flow gently around obstacles and when to gather force toward a shared purpose. Trust often grows in this quieter register. People do not commit because they are pushed; they commit because they feel understood, respected, and invited into something larger than themselves.

title: The Five Elements of Leadership
text:
Fire: Purpose That Gives Warmth
Fire is the element of energy, conviction, and illumination. It is what allows leadership to become more than management. A leader with Fire carries a sense of purpose that others can feel. In difficult moments, that sense of purpose can restore courage, helping a team remember why the work matters at all.
Yet Fire must do more than dazzle. The best leaders do not simply burn brightly; they generate warmth. Their passion does not consume the people around them. It animates them. It offers light in moments of ambiguity and helps others see a direction worth moving toward.

title: The Five Elements of Leadership
section: Earth: The Ground Where Others Grow
text:
Earth: The Ground Where Others Grow
Earth reminds us that leadership is not only about vision but also about holding space. Teams do their best work when they have stability, psychological safety, and a shared sense of grounding. A leader shaped by Earth creates that environment deliberately.
To lead in this way is to become a source of support rather than control. It means building a culture where people can experiment, make mistakes, and still feel held by a larger field of trust. Innovation rarely grows in fear. It grows in soil that is rich enough to hold risk, patience, and care.

title: The Five Elements of Leadership
text:
Wood: Growth, Direction, and Shelter
Wood is the element of emergence. It reaches upward, branches outward, and stays rooted at the same time. In leadership, Wood is vision joined with development. It asks not only where we are going, but who we are becoming as we move.
An inspiring leader creates pathways for others to stretch into their own capacities. They connect people, ideas, and opportunities. They offer direction without narrowing possibility. Like a strong tree, they provide both reach and shelter: enough structure to guide, enough openness for new forms of growth to appear.

title: The Five Elements of Leadership
text:
Closing reflection
If leadership can be understood through these five elements, then its deepest work is not domination but cultivation. It is the shaping of conditions in which clarity, trust, courage, stability, and growth can coexist. The leader is not the center of the system; the leader helps the system become more alive.
That, to me, is what it means to trust and inspire. It means leading in a way that helps other people become more fully themselves—more capable, more generous, more grounded, and more ready to carry the work forward.
